perturbation.original_text: Proprietary Information which may be supplied and is protected under this Agreement includes, but is not limited to: all business plans, specifications, reports, manuals, data books, computer programs, techniques, employment arrangements, and all other business practices or information of a private nature on any media whatsoever, and any derivatives of the foregoing, including, without limitation, any notes, analyses, compilations, studies, memoranda or other documents prepared by the Recipient, or its Representatives, which contain, reflect or are based on, in whole or in part, such proprietary information (collectively, "Proprietary Information"). Such Proprietary Information includes data transferred in the form of, but not limited to, oral, written, graphic or computer media including telephone, and meeting conversations, as well as all analyses, compilations, forecasts, studies or other documents which include any Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Proprietary Information which may be supplied and is protected under this Agreement includes only the specifically marked documents as 'Confidential' by the Disclosing Party, and delivered physically. Such information encompasses business plans, specifications, and reports directly related to the transaction under consideration. For avoidance of doubt, this excludes manuals, data books, computer programs, techniques, employment arrangements, and other business practices or information, including any derivatives such as notes, analyses, or compilations unless explicitly marked and physically handed over. Proprietary Information includes data transferred exclusively in written form, excluding oral, graphic or computer media.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a much narrower definition of 'Proprietary Information' in Section 2. The original definition was broad, encompassing various forms of information. The modified definition restricts it to explicitly marked documents physically delivered. This creates an in-text contradiction with the original, broader scope described in subsequent sections like Section 3 and Section 4, where all Proprietary Information is subject to protection and return, regardless of form or explicit marking. The phrase "as well as all analyses, compilations, forecasts, studies or other documents which include any Proprietary Information" is directly contradicted by the new restriction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Recipient agrees to keep confidential or protect such Proprietary Information as it would its own confidential and proprietary information at the highest level of proprietary material. Disclosure of such Proprietary Information shall be restricted to those Representatives of Recipient who (i) have a need to know the information for the purpose of evaluating a possible Business Arrangement between the parties and (ii) are informed by the Disclosing Party of the confidential and proprietary nature of the Proprietary Information. The Disclosing Party will cause its Representatives to observe the terms of this Agreement and the Disclosing Party will be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Proprietary Information,' limiting it to explicitly marked and physically delivered documents, while the contradicted text requires the recipient to protect 'such Proprietary Information' at the highest level, regardless of form or marking. The changed text's explicit exclusion of data transferred in oral, graphic, or computer media directly contradicts the contradicted text's implied inclusion of all forms of Proprietary Information. This creates a strong in-text contradiction because the protection obligation in the contradicted text would be unenforceable for information not meeting the new, stricter definition.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees to keep confidential or protect such Proprietary Information as it would its own confidential and proprietary information at the highest level of proprietary material. Disclosure of such Proprietary Information shall be restricted to those Representatives of Recipient who (i) have a need to know the information for the purpose of evaluating a possible Business Arrangement between the parties and (ii) are informed by the Disclosing Party of the confidential and proprietary nature of the Proprietary Information. The Disclosing Party will cause its Representatives to observe the terms of this Agreement and the Disclosing Party will be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees to protect Proprietary Information with a reasonable level of care. Disclosure of such Proprietary Information is permitted to all Representatives of the Recipient, provided they are informed of the general sensitivity of the information. The Disclosing Party is not responsible for breaches by Representatives of the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the obligation to protect Proprietary Information in Section 3. The original text mandates 'highest level of proprietary material' protection. The modified version lowers this to 'reasonable level of care' and allows disclosure to all representatives, undermining the original intent of restricted access and confidentiality and making it contradictory. It directly contradicts the final sentence, shifting responsibility of breach to the Recipient.
perturbation.contradicted_text: It is further understood and agreed that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by Recipient and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including injunctions and orders for specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach or threatened breach. Such remedy shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedy for breach of this Agreement but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity to Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the contradicted text. The original text and explanation state the Recipient is responsible for breaches by Representatives and the Disclosing party is entitled to equitable relief for any such breach, while the changed text states that the Disclosing Party is not responsible for such breaches. This creates a direct contradiction regarding liability for breaches, making the original agreement terms unenforceable and logically inconsistent.

perturbation.original_text: All Proprietary Information, including all applicable intellectual property rights residing in the Proprietary Information (including without limitation, patents, copyrights, industrial designs, trademarks and trade secrets), unless otherwise specified in writing by Disclosing Party, are and shall remain the exclusive property of Disclosing Party, including any and all reproductions in any form of said Proprietary Information. All such Proprietary Information (whether prepared by the Recipient or its Representatives on its behalf) shall be returned or destroyed, at the election of Disclosing Party or upon request of Disclosing Party provided, however, that all notes, analyses, conditions, studies, interpretations, memoranda or other documents, to the extent that they contain, reflect or are based upon the Proprietary Information, may be destroyed (rather than delivered); provided further that Board or Board Committee minutes need neither be delivered nor destroyed. Upon the request of Disclosing Party, Recipient shall execute a certificate of destruction of such Proprietary Information when destroyed by Recipient, whether intentionally or accidentally, and transmit the same to Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: All Proprietary Information remains the property of the Disclosing Party unless the Recipient enhances or modifies it; any enhancements or modifications become the Recipient's property. The Recipient is only required to return the original physical documents provided and is not required to destroy copies, analyses, or derivative works created by the Recipient. A certificate of destruction is only required for the originally supplied physical documents if requested by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change in Section 4 introduces a conflicting property right provision. The original text states all Proprietary Information remains the Disclosing Party's property. The modification allows the Recipient to own enhancements, creating an internal contradiction, and it contradicts the need to return or destroy all derivatives, and reduces it to only the originally supplied physical documents. This undermines intellectual property protection and the scope of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Recipient hereby agrees that it will not use the Proprietary Information for any reason or purpose other than to evaluate a possible Business Arrangement with the Disclosing Party and that the use of the Proprietary Information supplied hereunder shall be limited to Recipient's activities in connection therewith. For greater certainty nothing contained in this Agreement will be construed as granting or conferring upon the Recipient or its Representatives, any rights by license or otherwise, for any invention, discovery or improvement made, conceived or acquired, prior to or after the date of this Agreement based in whole, or in part, upon Proprietary Information provided to the Recipient or its Representatives hereunder.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 4 introduces a contradiction with the Contradicted Text. The Contradicted Text states that nothing in the Agreement will be construed as granting the Recipient any rights for improvements based on the Disclosing Party's Proprietary Information. However, the changed Section 4 states that enhancements or modifications to the Proprietary Information become the Recipient's property. This is a direct conflict, as it grants the Recipient property rights over enhancements, contradicting the original intent that all rights related to the Proprietary Information remain with the Disclosing Party, and violates the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that Recipient violates this Agreement or Disclosing Party has reasonable cause to feel that its Proprietary Information is in danger of being disclosed or misused, either Party may at any time, in addition to all other recourse under law, terminate this Agreement and demand and be entitled to the return from Recipient of all Proprietary Information in all existing forms.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that Recipient demonstrates gross negligence or willful misconduct leading to disclosure or misuse of Proprietary Information that results in direct, measurable financial harm to the Disclosing Party, the Disclosing Party may terminate this Agreement. However, termination is only permissible after a 30-day cure period where the Recipient attempts to rectify the situation. The demand for the return of Proprietary Information is waived.
perturbation.explanation: This change in Section 8 makes termination more difficult and removes the demand for return of Proprietary Information. Originally, termination was possible upon any violation or reasonable fear of misuse. Now, it requires gross negligence, provable financial harm, and a 30-day cure period. This is contradictory by limiting the right of the Disclosing Party to terminate the agreement, and explicitly contradicts the original agreement terms that required return of Proprietary Information in any form.
perturbation.contradicted_text: It is further understood and agreed that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by Recipient and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including injunctions and orders for specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach or threatened breach. Such remedy shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedy for breach of this Agreement but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity to Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 8 significantly alters the conditions for termination and waives the return of Proprietary Information. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that the Disclosing Party is entitled to equitable relief, including injunctions and orders for specific performance, as a remedy for *any* breach or threatened breach. The original text provided the Disclosing Party with greater rights, while the changed text limits those rights and removes the return of proprietary information, which is a direct contradiction. The original text allows for a variety of remedies for *any* breach, where the changed text creates specific conditions.
